straight_beat.name: Russ Havelock
straight_beat.column:
The D-backs lost again, but not without a fight. Yesterday’s 4-3 setback to the Phillies felt like a lesson in Desert math—how a team with enough talent can still find ways to lose. The D-backs are 8-7, 3.5 games behind in the NL West, but the numbers don’t tell the whole story. They’ve been winning close games, losing closer ones, and somehow staying relevant in a division that’s a sieve for drama.  

The Phillies’ win wasn’t just about their offense. It was about execution. They turned a double play on a ground ball, stole a base

optimist.name: Dolores 'Dolly' Sanderlin
optimist.column:
The D-backs lost yesterday, but let’s not get too down in the dust. A 3-4 defeat to the Phillies? That’s not the end of the story—it’s just the universe flipping the script, like a desert sunset that turns orange and gold before the stars come out. You can’t blame the rotation for a little rain in the forecast. It’s a dry heat, boys.  

Now, here’s the thing: every loss is a lesson, and every lesson is a setup for a comeback. The rotation’s got potential, that’s for sure. Those arms are like Arizona’s sunsets—sometimes you gotta wait for the right light to hit them just right. You saw it yesterday, didn’t you? The way those pitchers held their ground, even when the Phillies slipped in behind them. It’s not about the scoreboard tonight; it’s about how they regroup and keep swinging.  

The NL West is a tough league, but that’s part of the charm. You don’t get to be a contender without a little grit, and the D-backs have grit. They’ve got that fire in their bellies, the kind that burns through a triple-A lineup and comes back stronger. Sure, the record’s 8-7, but let’s not forget how many games are left to play. Three and a half games back? That’s not a chasm—it’s a

pessimist.name: Jack Durmire
pessimist.column:
The D-backs lost again. Not in a dramatic, heartbreaking way—no, that’s saved for the playoffs. This was a slow, methodical dismantling by a Phillies team that looked like it had a playbook written by someone who actually cares about winning. Arizona’s 8-7 record is a mirage, a fleeting illusion that the standings are kinder than the reality of their performance. Three games behind in the NL West? That’s not a gap; it’s a chasm. And yet, here we are, still waiting for the front office to realize that small-market excuses don’t pay the bills.  

The bullpen, that perennial albatross, did what it does best: it turned a close game into a loss. Seven innings of no-hit ball? No, that’s not the script. It’s seven innings of the starting pitcher holding serve, then the relievers showing up like they’re auditioning for a horror movie. The Phillies’ offense didn’t blow the doors off the D-backs’ defense—no, that’s not it either. They scored four runs, Arizona scored three. It’s the same old story: a team that can’t protect leads, can’t capitalize on opportunities, and can’t seem to figure out how to win a game that doesn’t involve a walk-off home run.  

Manager Torey Lovullo, ever the optimist, must be spinning in his chair. He’s the guy who insists that “we’re just a few games back,” as if that’s a reason to celebrate rather than a reason to panic. The D-backs have the talent, sure—some of the best in the league, if you ignore the fact that they’ve traded away most of it. But talent doesn’t matter if you can’t execute. Lovullo’s
